request.question: Whom did Rosko Gee play with on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005)?

title: Saw Delight
The Last Great Traffic Jam
Steve Winwood
When the Eagle Flies
Rosko Gee
Probka
Traffic wave
Gold (Traffic album)
China National Highway 110 traffic jam
Aizawl Khawpui Traffic Jam Hi
sentences: Saw Delight is the ninth Can studio album, and features two new band members who were ex-members of the band Traffic, Rosko Gee and Rebop Kwaku Baah, with Can's bassist Holger Czukay giving up the bass in favour of experimental effects.
The Last Great Traffic Jam is a live album and DVD from the rock band Traffic.
The album was recorded from the 1994 Traffic reunion concert tour.
Stephen Lawrence "Steve" Winwood (born 12 May 1948) is an English musician whose genres include rock, blue-eyed soul, rhythm and blues, blues rock, pop rock, and jazz.
Though primarily a vocalist and keyboardist, Winwood also plays bass guitar, drums, acoustic and electric guitar, mandolin, violin, and other strings.
When the Eagle Flies is the seventh studio album released by English rock band Traffic, in 1974.
It was the final album released by the band until their 1994 reunion "Far from Home".
The album featured Jim Capaldi on drums, keyboards and vocals; Rosko Gee on bass guitar; Steve Winwood on guitar, keyboards, and vocals; and Chris Wood on flute and saxophone.
(Percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah was fired prior to the album's completion, but his contributions are heard on two of its tracks.)
The album uses a broader variety of keyboard instruments than previous Traffic albums, adding Moog to their repertoire.

Rosko Gee is a Jamaican bassist, who has played with the English band Traffic on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005); with Go featuring Stomu Yamashta, Steve Winwood, Michael Shrieve, Klaus Schulze and Al Di Meola; and with the German band Can, along with former Traffic percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah, appearing on the albums "Saw Delight", "Out of Reach" and "Can".
He toured with Can in 1977, whereas Rebop did not.
He also provided vocals for some of the band's songs during this period.
"Probka" (Russian Cyrillic: Пробка; English translation: Traffic Jam) is a song by Latvian singer Intars Busulis, and was the Latvian entry at the Eurovision Song Contest 2009 in Moscow, Russia.
The song competed in the second semi-final of the contest on 14 May 2009 but failed to reach the final, having finished last with 7 points.
The song is unusually rhythmically complex for a Eurovision song, with the main verse section played in the unusual time signature of 7/4, and constant changes in tempo.
Busulis described "Probka" as "a song about a traffic jam in all senses and meanings.
We all have experienced in our lives that we just hurry, rush somewhere and at the certain moment there is a stop, jam or "probka".
That’s it – there is no way out, we are stuck!
This is an appropriate moment to think if it was worth it to run and hurry at all."
Traffic waves, also called stop waves or traffic shocks, are traveling disturbances in the distribution of cars on a highway.
Traffic waves travel backwards relative to the cars themselves.
Relative to a fixed spot on the road the wave can move with, or against the traffic, or even be stationary (when the waves moves away from the traffic with exactly the same speed as the traffic).
Traffic waves are a type of traffic jam.
A deeper understanding of traffic waves is a goal of the physical study of traffic flow, in which traffic itself can often be seen using techniques similar to those used in fluid dynamics.
Traffic Gold is a two-disc 2005 compilation album by the psychedelic rock band Traffic.
It contains at least one song from each album except "On the Road", "Far from Home", and "The Last Great Traffic Jam".

The China National Highway 110 traffic jam was a recurring massive traffic jam that began to form on August 14, 2010, mostly on China National Highway 110 (G110) and Beijing–Tibet expressway (G6), in Hebei and Inner Mongolia.
The traffic jam slowed down thousands of vehicles for more than 100 km and lasted for more than ten days.
Many drivers were able to move their vehicles only 1 km (0.6 mi) per day, and some drivers reported being stuck in the traffic jam for five days.
It is considered to be one of the longest traffic jams by some media.
Aizawl Khawpui Traffic Jam Hi is a 2017 Indian Mizo-language documentary film narrated, produced and directed by C. Hmingthangvunga.
The film tells about the traffic jam in Aizawl, the capital of Mizoram State in India.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: Whom did Rosko Gee play with on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005)?
hop1_docs: Rosko Gee | Rosko Gee is a Jamaican bassist, who has played with the English band Traffic on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005); with Go featuring Stomu Yamashta, Steve Winwood, Michael Shrieve, Klaus Schulze and Al Di Meola; and with the German band Can, along with former Traffic percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah, appearing on the albums "Saw Delight", "Out of Reach" and "Can".  He toured with Can in 1977, whereas Rebop did not.  He also provided vocals for some of the band's songs during this period.

When the Eagle Flies | When the Eagle Flies is the seventh studio album released by English rock band Traffic, in 1974.  It was the final album released by the band until their 1994 reunion "Far from Home".  The album featured Jim Capaldi on drums, keyboards and vocals; Rosko Gee on bass guitar; Steve Winwood on guitar, keyboards, and vocals; and Chris Wood on flute and saxophone.  (Percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah was fired prior to the album's completion, but his contributions are heard on two of its tracks.)  The album uses a broader variety of keyboard instruments than previous Traffic albums, adding Moog to their repertoire.
The Last Great Traffic Jam | The Last Great Traffic Jam is a live album and DVD from the rock band Traffic.  The album was recorded from the 1994 Traffic reunion concert tour.

Gold (Traffic album) | Traffic Gold is a two-disc 2005 compilation album by the psychedelic rock band Traffic.  It contains at least one song from each album except "On the Road", "Far from Home", and "The Last Great Traffic Jam".

Far from Home (Traffic album) | Far From Home, released in 1994, is the eighth and final studio album by the rock band Traffic, and the first in two decades since the release of "When the Eagle Flies" in 1974.  It was recorded at a large house called Woodstock, outside Kilcoole to the south of Dublin, and mixed at the Chateau Miraval in Correns, southern France.

Saw Delight | Saw Delight is the ninth Can studio album, and features two new band members who were ex-members of the band Traffic, Rosko Gee and Rebop Kwaku Baah, with Can's bassist Holger Czukay giving up the bass in favour of experimental effects.

To Whom It May Concern (Bee Gees album) | To Whom It May Concern is a 1972 album by the Bee Gees.  Released in October 1972, it was the follow-up, and continued the melancholic and personal sound of its predecessor "Trafalgar".  The album was recognized as "a farewell to the old Bee Gees" as the album marked the end of an era for the group in several ways: it was their last album to be recorded at IBC Studios, in London, their last with conductor and arranger Bill Shepherd who had guided them since 1967, and their last under their first contract with Robert Stigwood.  Some of the songs were old ones finished up or rewritten for the occasion (in the case of "I Can Bring Love").  "To Whom It May Concern" has sold approximately 350,000 copies worldwide.
hop1_titles: To Whom It May Concern (Bee Gees album)
response.summary_1:
Evidence: passages state Rosko Gee "has played with the English band Traffic" on those albums.  
Bridge entity: Traffic (the English rock band).  
Missing fact: the full personnel (which individual band members or guest musicians appeared with Rosko Gee on each specific album) is needed to list exactly whom he played with and is not fully enumerated for both albums in the supplied passages.  
Retrieval directions: (1) open the Wikipedia page for Traffic and the specific album pages "When the Eagle Flies" and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" to view the personnel/liner notes; or search '"When the Eagle Flies" personnel site:wikipedia.org' and '"The Last Great Traffic Jam" personnel site:wikipedia.org'.  
Clarification: none needed — the band identity (Traffic) is unambiguous in the passages.
response.hop2_query:
site:wikipedia.org "When the Eagle Flies" Traffic personnel OR "When the Eagle Flies (Traffic)" personnel
site:wikipedia.org "The Last Great Traffic Jam" personnel OR "The Last Great Traffic Jam (Traffic)" personnel
hop2_docs: Rosko Gee | Rosko Gee is a Jamaican bassist, who has played with the English band Traffic on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005); with Go featuring Stomu Yamashta, Steve Winwood, Michael Shrieve, Klaus Schulze and Al Di Meola; and with the German band Can, along with former Traffic percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah, appearing on the albums "Saw Delight", "Out of Reach" and "Can".  He toured with Can in 1977, whereas Rebop did not.  He also provided vocals for some of the band's songs during this period.
The Last Great Traffic Jam | The Last Great Traffic Jam is a live album and DVD from the rock band Traffic.  The album was recorded from the 1994 Traffic reunion concert tour.

Air traffic safety electronics personnel | Air traffic safety electronics personnel (ATSEP) is an ICAO term for the technical staff involved with the creation and support of the ground-based electronic hardware and software systems used to support air navigation and Air Traffic Management.

Gold (Traffic album) | Traffic Gold is a two-disc 2005 compilation album by the psychedelic rock band Traffic.  It contains at least one song from each album except "On the Road", "Far from Home", and "The Last Great Traffic Jam".

China National Highway 110 traffic jam | The China National Highway 110 traffic jam was a recurring massive traffic jam that began to form on August 14, 2010, mostly on China National Highway 110 (G110) and Beijing–Tibet expressway (G6), in Hebei and Inner Mongolia.  The traffic jam slowed down thousands of vehicles for more than 100 km and lasted for more than ten days.  Many drivers were able to move their vehicles only 1 km (0.6 mi) per day, and some drivers reported being stuck in the traffic jam for five days.  It is considered to be one of the longest traffic jams by some media.

Traffic guard | A traffic guard, traffic controller, flagman, or flagger is a person who directs traffic through a construction site or other temporary traffic control zone past an area using gestures, signs or flags.  The person directing traffic is responsible for maintaining the safety and efficiency of traffic, as well as the safety of road workers, while allowing construction, accident recovery or other tasks to proceed.  Traffic guards are commonly used to control traffic when two way roads are reduced to one lane, and traffic must alternate.  Their duties are to direct traffic to safer areas where construction or traffic incidents are taking place.  In addition they have to moderate the traffic density to not cause traffic jams.  They guide motorists to follow the traffic laws; but may not be able to enforce the law.  Most traffic guards are seen as construction workers; but in some nations, they dress or perform as security guards and police officers.

Air traffic management | Air traffic management is an aviation term encompassing all systems that assist aircraft to depart from an aerodrome, transit airspace, and land at a destination aerodrome, including air traffic control (ATC), air traffic safety electronics personnel (ATSEP), aeronautical meteorology, air navigation systems (aids to navigation), Air Space Management (ASM), Air Traffic Services (ATS), and Air Traffic Flow Management (ATFM), or Air Traffic Flow and Capacity Management (ATFCM).
response.summary_2: Rosko Gee played with the English rock band Traffic on the albums When the Eagle Flies (1974) and The Last Great Traffic Jam (2005). The Last Great Traffic Jam is a live album/DVD drawn from Traffic’s 1994 reunion tour, so his appearance on the 2005 release reflects those reunion performances.
